one_line_takeaway: A component-based taxonomy of twelve MCP-server attack categories backed by PoC servers that reach 100% attack success on six categories across frontier host-LLM combinations, while two popular scanners detect almost none - reframing the MCP server itself as a primary, easily mass-produced, hard-to-detect threat actor.
key_finding: Malicious MCP servers are broadly effective, easy to build at scale, and hard to detect. Six of the twelve PoC servers (A2 config abuse, A3 init-logic, A5 tool-logic, A6 tool-output, A8 resource-logic, A11 prompt-logic) achieved 100% ASR across ALL host-LLM combinations, showing hosts do not validate config files, do not check that server behavior matches declared functionality, and do not filter tool outputs (results are injected directly into the LLM context and relayed to users). Attack success depends jointly on host design, LLM safety training, and user awareness: e.g., swapping fast-agent's default system prompt for Cursor's dropped A4 (tool metadata poisoning) ASR from 100% to 6.7%, and GPT-4o resisted A4 on Cursor (0%) while other models did not. Reasoning models did not consistently offer better protection. On detection, both scanners were largely insufficient: mcp-scan (Invariant Labs, data-flow + pattern matching) detected only 4/10 servers, all in the A4 tool-description category and nothing else; AI-Infra-Guard (Tencent, LLM-agent based) did better on config/code-level attacks (A3 10/10, A8 10/10, A11 10/10, A2 8/10, A5 7/10) but failed on subtle text-manipulation attacks (A1 0/10, A6 2/10, A7 0/10, A12 0/10) and is slow/costly (~$0.50 and ~10 min per scan). mcp-scan also must launch the server, so init-logic malware runs before the scan finishes.
core_contribution: The paper presents what the authors describe as the first systematic study that treats Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers themselves as active threat actors, rather than treating the server as a benign channel through which external prompt-injection flows. Its central contribution is a component-based attack taxonomy: the authors decompose an MCP server into six components (metadata, configuration, initialization logic, tools, resources, prompts) and further into finer-grained elements, yielding twelve attack categories A1-A12 (e.g., A1 Server Metadata Poisoning, A2 Server Configuration Abuse, A3 Initialization Logic Attack, A4/A5/A6 Tool Metadata/Logic/Output Attacks, A7/A8/A9 Resource attacks, A10/A11/A12 Prompt attacks), each with multiple concrete sub-variants (e.g., rug pull, control-flow hijack, sampling abuse, endpoint exposure, phishing). On top of the taxonomy the paper adds two empirical contributions the authors claim distinguish them from prior work (Tab.1): (i) a cross-host AND cross-LLM effectiveness evaluation using twelve proof-of-concept malicious servers, and (ii) a scanner-effectiveness evaluation showing existing MCP security scanners largely fail to detect these servers. They also build a generator that mass-produces malicious servers from modular seed pools to argue the attacks are cheap and scalable.
threat_model: An adversary develops and publishes a malicious MCP server on a publicly accessible distribution platform (GitHub, npm, or dedicated MCP hubs like Smithery.ai, MCP.so, Glama that lack robust auditing). A benign user is enticed by an appealing name/description/config block and integrates it into their agent workflow per the provided instructions. Attacker GOALS: (i) compromise the local execution environment where the server runs, (ii) manipulate the LLM's behavior, or (iii) deceive the human user. Attacker CAPABILITIES: full control over the server codebase and all public-facing materials (README/markdown docs, config blocks); the attacker does NOT violate the protocol and may even use the official SDK. All OTHER parties are assumed benign and correct: the LLM is not adversarially trained or backdoored, users act in good faith, and the MCP host and client are correctly implemented and free of malicious intent. This is an important scoping choice - the paper isolates the server-as-attacker vector rather than assuming a compromised host or a jailbroken model.
limitations: The authors' own stated scope leaves several gaps. (1) PoC servers are installed locally and evaluated individually, so multi-server interactions and remote-server deployment behaviors are only reasoned about (Workflow 1), not measured. (2) ASR is over 15 trials per pair, a modest sample given LLM output variance, and 'intended effect achieved' is a manual/qualitative judgment for several categories. (3) Two attack categories are excluded from the main results table (A7 measured separately at 66.7% avg ASR; A10 not quantified because its impact 'depends on user awareness'), so the taxonomy's coverage in the empirical table is 10/12. (4) Only two scanners are tested; the authors chose them for popularity and methodological diversity, but conclusions about 'existing detection approaches' rest on this small sample. (5) Model/host versions are a snapshot (mid-2025 frontier models) and results will drift as safety tuning and host defenses change - indeed the paper shows outcomes are highly sensitive to system prompts. (6) The mitigations in Section 5 are a stakeholder responsibility framework (platforms, hosts, LLMs, users), not implemented or evaluated defenses. (7) PoC and generator code was 'publicly accessible upon acceptance' - not verified at review time.

datasets_used: 12 hand-built proof-of-concept malicious MCP servers (one per attack category A1-A12)
120 generator-produced malicious MCP servers (10 per attack category) used for the scanner evaluation
Generator seed pools: 5 malicious launch commands, 7 malicious initialization code snippets, 10 malicious + 30 benign tools, 10 malicious + 10 benign resources, 5 malicious + 5 benign prompts
Contextual figure: over 16K MCP servers publicly available online by August 2025 (cited, not collected by the authors)
quantitative_results: Six PoC servers (A2, A3, A5, A6, A8, A11) achieved 100% ASR across all host-LLM combinations
A7 (evaluated separately across 5 LLMs) average ASR 66.7%: GPT-4o 100%, Gemini-2.5-pro 100%, Claude Sonnet 4 66.7%, Claude Opus 4 53.3%, OpenAI o3 13.3%
Replacing fast-agent's system prompt with Cursor's dropped A4 ASR from 100% to 6.7%; GPT-4o on Cursor resisted A4 at 0%
A12 varied sharply by host: Claude Sonnet 4 @ Claude Desktop 93.3% vs @ fast-agent 0%; Claude Opus 4 @ Claude Desktop 73.3% (lower than Sonnet, consistent with stronger safety tuning)
Each attack run 15 times per host-model pair in fresh conversations
mcp-scan detection: 4/10 on A4, 0/10 on every other category
AI-Infra-Guard cost ~$0.50 and ~10 minutes per scan
Generator scale example: 10 tools and 10 resources yield up to (2^10 - 1)^2 = 1,046,529 unique servers
baselines_compared: mcp-scan (Invariant Labs) - data-flow-graph + pattern-matching scanner, selected for >=1k GitHub stars
AI-Infra-Guard (Tencent) - LLM-agent-based scanner (GPT-4o backbone), selected for >=1k GitHub stars

Prior MCP-security taxonomies/frameworks compared conceptually in Tab.1 (not run as baselines): Hou et al. [19], Jing et al. [21] (MCIP / MCIP-Bench), Narajala & Habler [27], and Guo et al. [18] (MCPLIB, 31 attacks across 4 families) - the paper claims it is the only one with both cross-host and scanner-effectiveness evaluation
